desc:
The Old East Road runs east to west, bordering the outskirts of the Chetwood
forest. To the south, the road is bounded by a hedge beyond which seems to be
open fields, which are mostly covered with grass and dotted with the occasional
small shrub.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Tucked between the hedge and the marsh, the Old East Road continues on from east
to west here, skirting the Midgewater to the north on its journey between
Breeland and rivendell. To the northwest the hardwood trees of the Chetwood
begin to become thicker, indicating that the ground is probably drier in that
direction.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The road is muddy here as it passes through a low depression. To the north, wet
grasses grow on a muddy plain -- a mere taste of the marshes that lie farther to
the northeast. The Great Hedge to the south blocks the South Downs from view,
but some distance to the southeast, a large hill peeks above it.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The Old East Road passes through a low area here, right on the southern edge of
the Midgewater Marshes. Somewhere to the east lie the Weather Hills -- the crown
of Weathertop is clearly visible when the sky is clear -- beyond which the road
passes on its long journey to Rivendell. To the west, the road bends north once
the marshes end, and then passes through the Chetwood and thence to Bree.
Alongside the southern edge of the road, the Great Hedge screens the empty hills
of the Southern Downs from view.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Just to the north of the road lie the treacherous fens of the Midgewater
Marshes. A low mist -- a seemingly eternal feature -- hangs low over the dense
vegetation, obscuring sight and hiding hordes of biting insects. Running
alongside the road to the south is the Great Hedge. In places it looms as much
as nine feet above the ground, and its tangled branches provide an unpassable
barrier between the marshland and road to the north and the expanse of the South
Downs to the south.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The road bends here once more, turning west to south to avoid the wet marshes.
To the east, a narrow strip of grassy ground provides a last chance of steady
footing before the marshes themselves. To the north, the weed-choked pools of
the Midgewater reach to the edge of the road itself.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Water running from the Old East Road to the south collects here, pooling and
collecting in little rivulets before running off into the Midgewater Marshes to
the north. Just south of the road can be seen the great Hedge, its branches so
intertwined that it is impossible to push through to the other side. Directly to
the north a low hill rises, allowing one a good view of the marshes.
name: Muddy Grasslands

desc:
The road bends east to north here, following the southern boundary of the
Midgewater. Pressed right against the roadside to the south is the Hedge --
somewhat thinner here than elsewhere, but still a non-trivial obstacle. On the
far side of the hedge, a large hill raises out of the landscape, and presumably
one could see much of the surrounding territory from that vantage point.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The road continues east and west, hemmed in by the Hedge to the south and the
Midgewater marshes to the north. Through a break in the reeds to the north, the
remnants of an ancient stone road can be seen, leading north into the marshes.
name: Old East Road

desc:
A small lake of black water lies just to the north, its dark water almost
lapping at the edge of the road. Another similar lake brushes the road a couple
of miles to the east. The Great Hedge to the south seems extraordinarily thick
here, as among the gnarled branches the straight trunks of many young oak trees
can be found, having found purchase on a small patch of higher ground.
name: Old East Road

desc:
To the north, a small lake of ominous black water encroaches to the fringe of
the Old East Road. The lake is surrounded by the reeds and sedges typical of the
Midgewater, and looks quite deep. To the south runs the Great Hedge, separated
the road from the South Downs, while east and west the Old East road continues
it journey.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The reeds of the Midgewater grow right beside the north edge of the road, and
the hard earth is muddy in several of the low-lying places. To the south, the
Hedge hides from view the wide open expanse of the South Downs.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The marshes to the north appear to end a short distance to the east, where the
land becomes higher and much drier. The Old East Road continues east and west,
and to the south lies the Hedge.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The faint smell of dank water lingers here at all times. This part of the road
is always pretty dark, as the hedge towers above it on the south and east sides.
The road makes a turn north and west here, barely avoiding the thorns and the
waters to the northwest. Deep ruts have been carved in the muddy earth, and this
has caused some wagon to loose a wheel which now lies half buried in the mud
just at the foot of the hedge.
name: Bend by the Hedge

desc:
This part of the road is in a bad shape. Muddy and deeply rutted it really could
use some attention. A faint effort to dig a ditch towards the marshes west of
here has proven a failure, and the ditch is full of black and still water. As
the road turns south it enters a thin stretch of land that lies between the
waters and the hedge. It continues in a fairly straight line east, eventually
disappearing below Weathertop on the south side. North of here lies some bushy
wetland, but beyond this the ground gets higher.
name: Bend by the Marshes

desc:
The hedge grows fairly low here on the south side of the road, about twelve feet
high only. Mounted people and trolls might attempt to peek over the edge and try
to see what is on the other side. Large footprints indicate that this has been
tried. The road is fairly bad shape, with ruts so deep as to make stones come
forth, causing wagons and cart to jolt.
name: Old East Road

desc:
As the road dodges a large rock north of here it pushes closer to the hedge. A
semi-arc of thorns cover half of the road, providing some shadow on hot days.
Now and then bypassers cut off twigs to keep the hedge at bay, and these litter
the ground. A faint trail runs north of the rock also, as some people prefer to
avoid this narrow passage.
name: Old East Road by a Rock

desc:
As well as the hedge grows on the south side of the road, bushes grow on the
north quite frequently. The road descends and rises fairly much for a few miles
here, and you see wild red and yellow flowers hiding amongst the bushes in the
dips. The road is in pretty good shape, and travelling is easy.
name: Old East Road

desc:
A few ashen trees form a grove on the north side of the road here. Someone has
cut down some of them, and the logs and stumps are used for resting and eating
on. A campfire has been assembled in the middle, and the place is littered by
bones and horse manure. You also see dog-like tracks all over. The hedge grows
thick south of here, and the road continues east and west.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Thick wooden beams support an archway through the hedge here on the south side
of the road. While burnt and battered, the beams still stand strong. The
handicraft is excellent, and surely made by numenorean hands. A path leads
through the opening to the plains south of the hedge. The brown Old East Road
runs away far both to the east and west, but eventually disappear south behind
the hedge in both directions. North of here lie vast grey plains beyond a small
grove of ashen trees.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The dusty road runs east-west here north of the thick hedge. To the north, vast
grey plains stretch out as far as the eye can see. Little wildlife catches the
eye, but you can hear birds and animals move inside the hedge.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The road makes a turn here, leaving in the south and west directions. The hedge
follows closely its path on the south side, being thorny and uncared for. It
ends a few yards from the road though, and poses no threat to travellers who
doesn't want to leave the road. All to the north and east scrubby plains stretch
out, and here and there the dark shadow of an old watchtower rise from them.
name: Bend by the Plains

desc:
Around this bend the thorns grow very high, towering to heights above twelve
yards. You can hear small animals play inside, and you spot a nest of some kind
high up. Northeast of here lies a patch of earth devoid of any signs of life.
You can follow the road north to another bend or east, where the road is stone-
laiden and well kept.
name: Bend by the Hedge

desc:
The hedge grows thick and pretty wild south of here, and the road turns and
twists fairly much to avoid its whims and spurs. On the north side of the road
the land is dead and brown, making a sharp contrast to the green and lively
hedge. For some reason the road is laden with stones on this part and eastwards,
while a mile further west it turns into a dustroad.
name: Old East Road

desc:
This part of the trail is one of those parts that shows its little use. Grass
grows along the trail and aside from the obvious cleared trail, if you were to
just look to the sides you would swear to be in outlands, far from any people
maybe though, this is best. Those lone travellers that seek isolation would be
enchanted by this land. Walking here with the little animals of the land making
their noises is so calm and peaceful that you could get used to it.
name: An Old Trail

desc:
There are few trees spread vast along this part of the trail. The grass looks to
be new. Only small creatures seem to be living in this area. This part of the
path seems to be slowly regrowing itself. It is like there once was a battle
here. The trail goes onward and is fairly easy to travel along.
name: A Small Trail

desc:
This trail is all but forgotten. The trees around here look to be dying. The
grass is brown, a few weeds survive. Dead animals lay along the trail. This
looks to be where a battle once had been fought. It is hard to tell just how
long ago it might have been, but judging from the corpses a couple years. This
is not a place to put up camp. The ground is scorched and hard.
name: The Forgotten Trail

desc:
The flowers along here are more like weeds. They have very little beauty in
them. The trail is overgrown with grass and in some areas mushrooms. This part
of the trail does not seem as well kept. Animals though can still be heard
making there way through the land. A flock of birds can be seen flying overhead.
name: An Overgrown Trail

desc:
This trail at times seem well kept, at others seems abandoned without any
reason. Giving you a strange feeling. The flowers along the trail here are
beautiful and seem to have been taken good care of. In other areas this does not
seem to be the case. What reason for this though you do not know.
name: A Small Trail

desc:
The path seems to be leading towards a bridge, this may be a way to cross the
river that can be heard in the distance. The land around here is level and seems
to be a nice place to set up camp off the main trail. Animals can be heard
moving towards the river, most likely to get a drink of water. Making your own
mouth water at the thought of it.
name: Near a Rickety Old Bridge

desc:
You are now over the river. Over to the east is the end of the bridge, and
safety. You could head out further on the bridge it looks safe enough to go on
forward. The wind is blowing slightly, causing the bridge to creak and rock in
the wind. Either way you go you might want to think of going that way fast.
name: Rickety Old Bridge

desc:
Below you is a small calm river that is not that far down. If you look closely
you can see what looks like bass and other things swimming. Moving on this
bridge is difficult and one at a time is a good idea. To the west you see the
end of the bridge and looking back east, you can see the far side swaying in the
breeze. Large planks of wood have been made in some parts of the bridge, making
it a whole lot more sturdy.
name: A Rickety Rope Bridge

desc:
You look out east and see what looks like a bridge across the river. You test
the bridge and it seems quite sturdy to support you. The river is quietly moving
along. Down south you see a path along the river. To the west is a little
overgrown trail it leads through the bushes. You can also go north along the
river, maybe there is somewhere along the bank to wade across as well. There
what looks like a path along the river to the north. You notice some extra
support along the sides of the bridge.
name: A Path to an Old Rope Bridge

desc:
The trail borders some hills, the hills seem to go on for quite some ways to the
north. The sounds of a small river can be heard not too far towards the east.
The trail is quite level here and easy to move along. The best thing to do would
be stay on the trail. The trees around the trail are small trees, easy to climb
and see what might be in them. If you had the time, you could rest up there in
one of the trees, the sun is nice fairly nice around this area.
name: Trail around the Hills

desc:
Hiking along here seems quite peaceful, a few birds sing in the trees around
you. A couple rabbits jump and scurry off as you crunch down on a few fallen
twigs. The ground is fairly even and only a few tree roots penetrate the ground
as you walk calmly along this trail.
name: A Small Trail Path

desc:
The path is very quiet, so quiet the air barely moves across the ground. The
grass and shrubs along the path are green and living, filling the still air with
their scent. There are few obstacles along the path, the ground is very level
and has few rocks along it.
name: A Dusty Path

desc:
This old trail runs along the bottom of a scarp that separates the plains to the
north from the lower lands around the Midgewater Marshes. In many places, deep
channels have been cut into the sandstone of the cliff and debris has been
deposited across the trail in alluvial fans.
name: Narrow Trail

desc:
Just to the north, a small but steep cliff rises alongside this trail, while to
the southwest, the land slopes gently downward to the fens of the Midgewater
Marshes. This trail is littered with debris that has washed down from the
plateau above, and in places has been almost obliterated.
name: Narrow Trail

desc:
This trail runs parallel to a small cliff that rises sharply to the level of the
dry plains to the northeast. The rain that falls on the grassy plateau drains
rapidly into the marshes to the west, for the scarp is cut in many places by
severe channels and gullies.
name: Narrow Trail

desc:
This trail runs at the foot of a small cliff that demarcates the boundary
between the high plains to the northeast and the depression that houses the
Midgewater Marshes to the southwest. The cliff is up to sixty feet high in
places, and its steep wall is too crumbly to scale.
name: Narrow Trail

desc:
Strewn with rocks that have washed down from the rocky plains above, this narrow
trail takes advantage of a small dry area along the foot of the scarp to avoid
the soggy plain to the west. The trail bends from east to north, hugging the
cliff all the way.
name: Trail below the Scarp

desc:
Following the contour dictated by the foot of the scarp to the north, the path
turns from west to south here. The scarp is higher to the east, reaching in some
places up to sixty feet above the level of the trail. The trail appears to begin
sloping gently upward to the east, finally leaving the marshes behind.
name: Bend in the Trail

desc:
A large gully cuts into the face of the scarp, leading sharply upward to the
north. The trail passes between the gully's mouth and the marsh to the south.
The gully itself is heavily vegetated with dense scrub, but it is possible to
take advantage of it and climb to the plateau above.
name: Mouth of Gully

desc:
The trail is littered with debris that has washed off the scarp to the north.
The scarp is cut with many small channels - too steep to climb - that indicate
that rainfall quickly drains from the plains to the north into the marshes to
the south.
name: Trail along the Marsh

desc:
This trail of packed earth runs along the northern boundary of the Midge- water,
closely following the foot of a scarp that forms the boundary for the high
plains to the north. At this point, the trail bends from north to east, nestled
in a small dry area between the scarp and marsh.
name: Trail along the Marsh

desc:
This trail bends from the west to the south, following the contours of the land.
It is bounded to the north and east by a steep scarp about thirty feet high that
forms the boundary of the lowlands inhabited by the Midgewater that lies off the
southern edge of the trail.
name: Trail along the Marsh

desc:
Cattails - some several feet high - obscure vision in every direction. The parts
of the ground that are not submerged beneath the black water consist of large
islands of mud that smell of rotting vegetation. The air is filled by the
cacophony of buzzing insects and singing frogs, and sometimes seems to be just a
solid wall of insect swarms.
name: Cattails

desc:
The ground is slightly drier here than to the southeast, but still damp enough
to support hordes of cattails. Their long stalks sprout everywhere and make it
difficult to see more than a few feet in any direction. Clouds of bothersome
insects rise at each footstep, eagerly seeking their next meal.
name: Cattails

desc:
To the west lies a wall of cattails, marking the edge of the Midgewater Marshes.
This area, although still damp, resembles the grassy plains found elsewhere on
Arda. To the east and north a narrow path can be made out, hugging the foot of a
tall scarp.
name: Trail below the Scarp

desc:
To the west, the grasses of this area give way to growths of sedges and reeds
that mark the Midgewater Marshes. The ground is very damp, and it seems to
gently slope down toward the marsh. The grassland is cut to the east by a trail
that runs along the bottom of an escarpment.
name: Wet Grasslands

desc:
To the east, the land rises slightly and the thick sedges and reeds give way to
grasses. There is little open water here - mostly the ground is made of thick
mud that smells of rotting vegetation. To the southwest, the Midgewater Marshes
make travel an almost impossible chore, but to the northeast, there appears to
be a trail that runs along the edge of the fens.
name: Edge of Marsh

desc:
There is little open water anywhere in this area - thick plant-life erupts
through the dark surface of the water, forming a veritable jungle of reeds and
sedges. Nowhere is the water more than a foot in depth, but the mud beneath the
surface sometimes seems bottomless.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
This area of the marsh is characterized by huge stands of cattails surrounding
large open stretches of mucky black water. Most of the ponds are too deep for
comfort, but there are enough shallow areas to travel further in any direction.
To the north, the cattails take over completely, and their long stalks block any
view of the areas in that direction.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
The clouds of insects that give these marshes their name are clearly in
evidence, with mosquitoes, gnats, and midges filling the air and generally
making nuisances of themselves. Where the water is shallow enough to allow
passage, the bottom is a thick black mud that pulls downward at each footstep.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
This area is criss-crossed by several shallow ditches filled with muddy water.
The water in the ditches is stagnant - if it flows at all it must drain into the
marsh. A low and muddy ridge to the north is home to a stand of willows, but
only sedges, reeds, and occasional cattails grow alongside the channels.
name: Muddy Ditches

desc:
A thick wall of reeds encircles this round pool of dark water. The lake does not
have well-defined boundaries, for beneath the cover of the reeds, the water and
thick mud seem to merge seamlessly, one into another.
name: Murky Lake

desc:
The ground is completely covered by a layer of water - mostly of depth less than
six inches, but in other places there seems to be no bottom. It is impossible to
travel consistently in a single direction, as the marshes seem to conspire to
make travel as tortuous as possible.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
The frequent stretches of deep mud and black ponds makes it impossible to travel
in a given direction with any certainty. On all sides the marshes reign
unchallenged, providing a home to ravenous hordes of biting and stinging
insects, who in turn sustain a huge population of frogs and other less familiar
predators.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
To the east, the thick vegetation of the marsh seems to abate slightly. Every
square inch of mud that lies above the waterline is home to chaotic tangles of
shrubs and reeds that intertwine in thick knots. The cover of the plant life
hides hordes of insects that rise in great black clouds as soon as the plants
are disturbed.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
To the west, the rampant vegetation of the marsh reigns unchallenged - from here
it looks like a wall of tangled reeds and sedges. To the north and east grasses
dominate the wet plain, while to the south the ground looks to be a huge flat
expanse of mud.
name: Edge of Marsh

desc:
The ground here is very damp, and often small muddy puddles greet careless
footsteps. Everything is overgrown with a variety of grass that is verdant green
with long, wide blades - almost like sawgrass, except without the serrated
edges.
name: Wet Grasslands

desc:
The ground beneath the thick grassy cover is actually quite dry, in stark
contrast to the wet mires that lie to the south and east. To the north runs a
small trail, hugging the bottom of the scarp that marks the northern boundary of
this basin.
name: Grasslands

desc:
A sluggish stream - bloated with mud - runs just south of here, emptying into
the marsh. The stream's bank is basically just a ridge of mud that has been
anchored by several small willow and alder trees. A few miles to the north, the
land rises abruptly in a steep scarp that marks the northern edge of the basin
occupied by the Midgewater Marshes.
name: Beside a Muddy Stream

desc:
A sluggish stream - more of a wide ditch than anything - lies just south of this
area, beyond a small stand of willow trees. Some distance to the north, a large
scarp looms, marking the southern boundary of a region of stark plains. The
vegetation here is thick, consisting mostly of many varieties of grass, but
occasionally a small tree has found a niche in which to flourish.
name: Beside a Muddy Stream

desc:
Running from east to west, this scarp overlooks a depression in the earth that
is now filled by the Midgewater Marshes. The rock of the scarp is generally very
crumbly, but there are patches of solid granite. The plains that extend far to
the north are dotted with great boulders that seem to have been dropped at
random. Farther to the east, it appears that the ground along the foot of the
scarp rises, and consequently is much drier.
name: Top of Scarp

desc:
To the north and east stretches an expanse of sparsely vegetated and rocky
plains. South and west, the plains come to an abrupt ending at a small cliff
that overlooks the marshlands below. The cliff is much higher to the west, where
the plains rise up to form a hill, but for the most part it is around twenty
feet high. Running at the bottom of the cliff is a small trail that can probably
be accessed from here, but the crumbly texture of the scarp edge suggests that
getting back up may be impossible without great effort.
name: Top of Scarp

desc:
To the west, the land rises into a large hill, while in every other direction,
nothing but more plains can be discerned. Frequently, boulders are scattered
about, like they had been carelessly tossed away by a gigantic child. Besides
the wiry grass that covers the hard soil, patches of thorny brush sometimes are
seen.
name: High Plains

desc:
The plains here form a hill whose southern and western slopes appear to have
been shorn off, leaving behind a sheer cliff about sixty feet from top to
bottom. Along the base of the cliff runs a small trail that leads around the
northern edge of the Midgewater, and then through the Chetwood into Bree-land.
name: High Point

desc:
The hard plains come to an end quickly to the southwest at a small cliff that
overlooks the Midgewater. Here on top of the scarp, the ground is mostly dry and
the only signs of life are patches of brush and occasional rabbits who scamper
into the open.
name: Bleak Plains

desc:
To the north and east, the land slopes upward to a broad plateau. These bleak
plains end immediately to the south at a small cliff that stands around thirty
feet high. At the foot of the scarp, a small trail skirts the northern edge of
the Midgewater, whose misty expanse makes up the landscape to the south. The
cliff is too steep to negotiate safely here.
name: Edge of Scarp

desc:
The steep scarp immediately to the south is here cut by a deep and narrow gully.
The bottom of the gully is choked with brush, and it appears to have been dry
for years. Following the floor of the gully to the south will lead one to the
narrow path that follows the foot of the scarp.
name: Brush-filled Gully

desc:
The plains to the north and east come to an abrupt end here on top of a steep
scarp. Beyond the foot of the scarp lies the wet expanse of the Midgewater
Marshes, but it seems there is a narrow trail running flush against the bottom
of the cliff. The drop is around thirty feet, and it looks to be possible to
slide down it to the trail below, although getting back up may be a problem.
name: Edge of Scarp

desc:
The few trees that have survived the buffeting winds of these plains have had
their growth stunted. The most successful plants are several varieties of tough
brush and thorn bushes that frequently cluster in impenetrable clumps.
name: Windswept Plains

desc:
Cold winds frequently buffet the hard ground, loudly rustling through the dense
low brush. There are very few trees, and the few that have managed to survive
have been twisted by the wind into squat and tortured shapes. The plain slopes
downward to the south, until it reaches a steep scarp that marks the edge of the
depression occupied by the Midgewater Marshes.
name: Windswept Plains

desc:
Two huge fingers of granite thrust upward out of the unforgiving soil, towering
twenty feet over the surrounding plateau. The edges of the rock are deeply
scored with grooves that resemble claw marks made by some tremendous, impossible
beast. Scattered about are large boulders of the same type of rock, also bearing
the same type of grooves.
name: Rocky Outcropping

desc:
The exposed rock that frequently emerges from the hard earth looks as if some
gigantic claw had raked it in the distant past, as the granite has been furrowed
in a consistently north-south fashion. The plains slope upward to the northeast
to form a broad plateau, and somewhere to the southwest lie the Midgewater
Marshes.
name: Bleak Plains

desc:
The plains are dotted with huge boulders that look as if they have been
carelessly strewn about. Dense brush grows on the hard soil that dominates the
area, although periodically the soil gives way to bare and windswept granite.
name: Bleak Plains

desc:
The land levels off here, forming a large plateau. To the southwest, it drops
steadily, descending into the depression occupied by the Midgewater Marshes.
There are little signs of life besides a few patches of brush and signs of
rabbits, as nothing large could subsist on the harsh landscape.
name: High Plains

desc:
This plateau supports scraggly patches of grass that become thicker to the east,
where water seems to be more plentiful. The landscape is dotted with large
boulders that seem to have been randomly scattered by some gigantic hand.
Occasional outcroppings of granite appears as well, mostly worn smooth and
having the appearance of having been scraped by huge claws.
name: High Plains

desc:
The land is eternally grey here, with cold hard earth and occasional patches of
exposed granite extending to the northeast all the way to the horizon. Sporadic
patches of spiny scrub offer little to relieve the monotony of the landscape,
but offer shelter from the wind for the small animals that survive out here.
name: Bleak Plains

desc:
The earth is dry and cold here, and very few plants manage to eke out an
existence here. Frequent winds gust from the north, chilling the air and hinting
of frigid wastes far to the north. To the west, a narrow and deep gully cuts
across the land, and to the north and east the land slopes upward, presumably
until the Weather Hills far to the east are reached.
name: Bleak Plains

desc:
A deep gully knifes across the plain here, leading south into the expanses of
marshes that lie some distance away. The bottom of the gully is completely
choked with brush, and apparently it has been years since water consistently
flowed through it. To the north and east, the bleak plains continue to the
horizon, offering nothing to break the monotony of the landscape.
name: Brush-filled Gully

desc:
To the north, an open lake can be spied beyond a wall of reeds. Everything here
is either wet or alive, making for a most uncomfortable journey. Each footstep
is an effort, as the muddy ground seems to be trying to ingest those foolish
enough to travel here.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
The frequent stretches of deep mud and black ponds make it impossible to travel
in a given direction with any certainty. On all sides the marshes reign
unchallenged, providing a home to ravenous hordes of biting and stinging
insects, who in turn sustain a huge population of frogs and other less familiar
predators.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
The frequent stretches of deep mud and black ponds makes it impossible to travel
in a given direction with any certainty. On all sides the marshes reign
unchallenged, providing a home to ravenous hordes of biting and stinging
insects, who in turn sustain a huge population of frogs and other less familiar
predators.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
This earthen mound rises a few feet above the level of the surrounding marsh. It
is completely surrounded by a wall of impenetrable reeds - the only exit from
the islet appears to be the trail leading west. A small shanty has been erected
on the eastern portion of the islet.
name: Hidden Islet

desc:
This ramshackle shanty has been carelessly thrown together out of spare scraps
of wood. There are many small gaps between boards, so that very little shelter
is provided from wind or rain. In the centre of the room, a small table sits on
the earthen floor, and a few chairs have been set around it.
name: Inside the Shanty

desc:
Lots of junk has been haphazardly scattered around this storage room - no care
at all has been taken, and much of the stuff is broken. The walls of this shabby
room are crudely fashioned - glimpses of the outside are visible through cracks
between the boards. The roof is in even worse shape, and this explains why a lot
of the equipment in here has been severely water damaged.
name: Back Room

desc:
This room has been hollowed out of the earth, and its walls are simply the damp
soil. The place smells as if animals have been kept penned in here, further
evidenced by the dung on the floor. Some old rags have been piled in one corner,
and in the other corner is an old chest.
name: Beneath the Floorboards

desc:
Pools of water hide treacherous depths of soupy mud, so each step must be
carefully planned before execution. Small flowering plants occasionally bloom
amongst the reeds and sedges, adding a bit of contrasting colour to the green
and black of the marshes.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
To the north and east, a large amount of mud has been deposited, burying all of
the vegetation. Here at the edge of the muddy flats, the reeds and sedges still
hold sway, though they are nowhere nearly as dense as in the depths of the
Midgewater Marshes, which begin in earnest to the south and west.
name: Edge of Marsh

desc:
The sticky black mud of this area smells vaguely of rotting vegetation. The
viscous sludge is in places over two feet in depth - it was probably deposited
recently because the plants of the marsh to the southwest have yet to colonise
here successfully.
name: Muddy Plain

desc:
Hordes of biting and stinging insects swarm through the thick air, feasting
mercilessly on every square inch of exposed flesh. Great care is required to
navigate successfully here amongst the fens, as bottomless pits of mire can lurk
beneath seemingly shallow pools.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
The frequent stretches of deep mud and black ponds makes it impossible to travel
in a given direction with any certainty. On all sides the marshes reign
unchallenged, providing a home to ravenous hordes of biting and stinging
insects, who in turn sustain a huge population of frogs and other less familiar
predators.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
The frequent stretches of deep mud and black ponds makes it impossible to travel
in a given direction with any certainty. On all sides the marshes reign
unchallenged, providing a home to ravenous hordes of biting and stinging
insects, who in turn sustain a huge population of frogs and other less familiar
predators.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
This vast expanse of sticky black mud was probably once an open grassland
similar to the landscape to the north, but apparently the stream to the east
overspilled its banks and deposited all this sediment. Sporadic patches of grass
poke their stems up through the muck, hinting that they will soon reclaim this
land.
name: Muddy Plain

desc:
This silt-laden stream nears its end here -- all hint of its channel vanishes
when it enters the marshes to the southwest. The stream's banks are low mounds
of sticky black mud, cemented into place by many small willows and alders.
name: Muddy Stream

desc:
This stream loses its coherence here, as all trace of a current disappears amid
these muddy flats. To the south and west, the amount of mud that has been
deposited hints that this stream recently flooded, leaving behind the thick
viscous mud that coats the plain. To the east, a stand of alders is flourishing
in the damp soil.
name: Muddy Stream

desc:
Just to the north, a sluggish stream dumps its load of mud into the marshes.
This area is almost devoid of vegetation -- it looks as if someone coated the
landscape with a layer of sticky black mud. A few tentative tendrils of green
have begun to show, and soon this plain will once again be home to the rampant
vegetation of the marsh.
name: Muddy Flats

desc:
The murky water of this pond seems to be very deep. Occasionally great bubbles
float to the surface and burst, smelling vaguely of rotting plants. The pond is
choked with plant life, and its muddy banks are home to a dense wall of reeds.
name: Dark Pond

desc:
Just to the east, a solid wall of reeds marks the edge of a stretch of open
water. This area is home to many insects - most of which seem intent on feeding
- that are a constant nuisance. The muddy ground is generally covered with
sedges and tangled brambles, while the frequent pools of dark water sport a coat
of water lilies and other aquatic plants.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
The sedges briefly give way to a large area of open water. The water is deep
brown and occasionally bubbles slowly work their way to the surface and burst --
probably gasses from decaying matter on the bottom. On all sides, the sedges
quickly reassert themselves, completely choking any exposed ground with their
tangled growths.
name: Muddy Pool

desc:
The dense reeds and rushes frequently hide pools of ominous-looking black water.
Insects are the masters here - they fill the air in great buzzing clouds, eager
for a meal. From time to time a splash of water can be heard, indicating that
other, larger forms of life are around.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
This flat plain is totally coated in mud, as it is a miniature delta for the
sluggish stream to the east. Small channels in the mud are filled with water
that seems to be slowly draining into the marsh. Very little vegetation has
grown here - perhaps the mud was deposited recently.
name: Muddy Flats

desc:
These trees anchor the southern bank of the sluggish stream that flows just to
the north - they have thrived here in the damp soil. The stand of alders
continues to the south, while the stream bends so that it also lies to the west
of here.
name: Alder Trees

desc:
These young alders have flourished here in the damp earth. They are common to
the north as well, while to the south a small tongue of higher ground that
protrudes into the marsh is home to a grove of young willows. To the west, all
vegetation has been buried beneath a thick layer of sticky mud.
name: Alder Trees

desc:
This tongue of hardened earth protrudes out into the marsh, almost as if the
plains to the east were attempting an invasion. Several young trees have taken
advantage of the soil and taken root - they would be unable to survive in the
saturated soil of the surrounding marshes.
name: Stand of Trees

desc:
A small tongue of high ground protrudes westward -- its crest overgrown with
young oak trees that have taken advantage of the dry ground and taken root. A
small spring of clear water bursts from the ground here, and its runoff flows
down the southern slope of the hill where it is quickly lost among the marshy
grassland.
name: Stand of Trees

desc:
To the west, a small tongue of higher ground protrudes into the marsh, providing
a home to a small stand of trees. The marsh here is home to many varieties of
sawgrass, and their serrated blades tend to cut anything with which they come in
contact.
name: Sedges

desc:
This is a dense stand of sedges with sharp, cutting leaves. White mist floats in
the air, lending an aura of mystery to the marsh. With each step, clouds of
insects are stirred up from the vegetation, eager for a meal.
name: Sedges

desc:
Many varieties of saw-toothed grass make this area their home. The ground is
saturated with water, and makes a squishing sound at each step. The monotonous
buzzing of insects continues incessantly, broken only by the calls of frogs and
occasional splashes of unknown origins.
name: Edge of Marshes

desc:
Beneath the grassy cover, many shallow pools of water are hidden. Most of them
are fairly muddy, but there seems to be a source of fresh water to the north
that keeps a few of them drinkable.
name: Marshy Grasslands

desc:
The ground here is very damp -- in some places as much as an inch of water lies
hidden under the waving grass. The terrain slopes slightly downward to the west,
so this water must drain into the marshes in that direction. Occasionally,
narrow rivulets wind through the grasses, their flow unconstrained by any
channel.
name: Marshy Grasslands

desc:
To the west, the ground sinks and becomes gradually damper, until at last the
grass of the plains is replaced with the rampant vegetation of the Midgewater
Marshes. Eastward lies a land of low and grassy hills, rolling unchallenged,
save for an occasional stand of trees, to the distant Weather Hills.
name: Plains

desc:
To the north and east, the ground becomes much drier as it slopes upward to the
Weather Hills many miles to the east. Westward, the ground descends into the
depression that is home to the Midgewater Marshes and their hordes of gnats and
midges. The ground here is damp, but there is very little standing water to be
found.
name: Wet Grassland

desc:
Each footstep is greeted by a soft squishing noise as the ground seems to be
saturated, even though there is very little standing water. The grasses of the
South Downs, which lie south beyond the Great Hedge, have here found a foothold
and flourished on the wet plain. Directly to the south, the Old East Road runs
along the north side of the Great Hedge.
name: Marshy Ground

desc:
Bounded on the east by marshy grasslands and on the west by an ominous looking
black pond, this strip of land is the transition from the plains that lie to the
east, and the marshes that lie to the north and west. To the south, the Old East
Road is tucked along the northern side of the Great Hedge as it skirts the
southern end of the marshes on its journey between Bree and Rivendell.
name: Marshy Ground

desc:
This pond of foul black water is bordered by thick growths of reeds and
cattails. When the bottom can be felt, it seems to be thick mud covered with
layers of rotting plant debris. Just to the south, the Old East Road passes, and
the water of this pond literally laps on the northern edge of the road.
name: Black Pond

desc:
This pond, and its twin lying a short distance to the east, are filled with
foul-smelling black water. The unmistakeable odour of decomposing plants
thickens the air, though the hordes of mosquitoes and other insects do not seem
to mind. When the bottom of the pond can be felt, it seems to be made of thick
mud covered with a layer of slimy rotting vegetation. To the north, the muddy
shore marks the beginning of the Midgewater, while on the south side runs the
Old East Road.
name: Black Pond

desc:
A few scattered stone blocks are all that remain of an ancient causeway, the
rest having been claimed by the marshes. It appears that a road once led north
here, but there is no hint as to its eventual destination. The Old East Road
bends around this wet area that is nestled between the Midgewater to the north
and a murky black lake to the east.
name: Edge of Marsh

desc:
The insects of the Midgewater seem to be under the guidance of a malevolent
intelligence, as they somehow seem able to find skin no matter how well covered.
The ground -- where it rises above the shallow water -- is com- pletely
saturated and the footing is often treacherous. To the south, the marshes seem
less severe, but this in every other direction the terrain is much the same.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
Just to the south, beyond the edge of the marshes, the Old East Road con- tinues
its journey between Bree and Rivendell. The wet marshes almost reach the road,
which is sandwiched between them and the Great Hedge that lies a bit farther
south. To the north, a low mist hangs over the marsh, hiding the hordes of
insects whose buzzing can be heard even now.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
The air is filled with insects that bite, sting, and generally do their best to
annoy. Each step farther into the marsh brings a chorus of sound from loud
insects hidden among the vegetation. Sometimes the frogs begin to call back and
forth, raising their voices to almost deafening levels.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
The maze of mud and vegetation of the heart of the marshes lies to the
northwest. Here it is still possible to move fairly easily, as the sedges and
reeds are not that dense. To the south, a pond of black water is nestled among
the reeds, while to the east the ground becomes drier as the edge of the marsh
nears.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
To the west, the marshes begin in earnest. This area consists of damp ground
overgrown with many varieties of sawgrass. The serrated blades of grass,
although not dangerous, are still capable of inflicting small stinging cuts on
the careless -- cuts that are immediately sought out by hordes of blood-drinking
insects.
name: Edge of Marsh

desc:
A low mist hangs over this area of the marsh, as the air is completely saturated
with water. Just to the south, right along the edge of the marshes, the Old East
Road passes. To the west, the terrain gets slightly drier, but to the north and
east, travel among the fens will be difficult.
name: Edge of Marsh

desc:
The marshes encroach from the north until the almost reach the Old East Road to
the south. This fringe of damp grasslands dotted with open pools will certainly
one day become part of the Midgewater Marshes to the north, but for now the land
still sustains many of the grasses that are characteristic of the downs to the
south of the Old East Road.
name: Edge of Marsh

desc:
The ground slopes steadily downward to the north, becoming steadily damper until
the fens of the Midgewater Marsh are reached. The Old East Road runs a short
distance to the south, skirting the southern end of the marshes.
name: Muddy Grasslands

desc:
The constant din of uncounted hordes of insects and amphibians at times seems
loud enough to drown out speech. The ground here is mostly soft mud that is at
times covered with up to a foot of brown water, making each step an
uncomfortable chore. Directly to the west, a low bluff marks the end of the
marsh in that direction at least.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
The soupy mud that greets each step makes travel an unpleasant experience. It is
nearly impossible to travel in a generally straight line -- any progress at all
is via circuitous and tortuous routes through the mazey mire.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
The muddy bottom beneath the murky water makes each footstep an effort, as if
the Midgewater were deliberately trying to devour prey. To the east, a damp mist
lies low over the marsh, and to the north lies a dense growth of sedges.
Westward the vegetation seems to thin a bit, though clouds of biting insects
seem unphazed by the lack of cover.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
Considerable effort is required to successfully navigate amongst the many pools
of black water, as the thick vegetation and grasping black mud seem to impede
each step with a malevolent intent. To the east, the vegetation clears and
reveals a large pool of what looks to be soupy mud, while in every other
direction, the marshes continue.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
The mud here is very deep, and it seems keen on enveloping anyone foolish enough
to tread here. Luckily, there are several twisting routes that avoid the deep
muck, although any travel must be done slowly and carefully.
name: Deep Mud

desc:
To the west is what appears to be a lake of soupy mud. On every other side, the
rampant vegetation of the marsh makes motion difficult. Clouds of insects and
the frogs that feed on them can be seen everywhere, and they fill the air with
noise.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
The spongy ground is saturated with water. A light mist caresses the marsh
plants with wispy white tendrils, and all around the hum of insects and the
songs of frogs can be heard.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
The ground is slightly drier here than to the west, and on the whole it appears
to be rising slightly to the east, where the towering Weather Hills can be spied
in the distance. The vegetation here runs from the sedges and reeds of the
marshes to the grasses of the plains to the east.
name: Wet Grasslands

desc:
The spongy ground is saturated with water. A light mist caresses the marsh
plants with wispy white tendrils, and all around the hum of insects and the
songs of frogs can be heard.
name: Edge of Marshes

desc:
Like the hump of some great sea serpent, this islet of spongy black mud rises a
short distance out of the murky mud. Completely overgrown with dense vegetation,
it is difficult to push through to the marsh on the other side. The air is
choked with gnats and midges, and the sonorous singing of the multitudes of
frogs and insects drones on incessantly. The slope to the south appears to be
very slick and steep, so it might be difficult climbing it back out of the
swamp.
name: Spongy Islet

desc:
The air is filled with the cacophony of millions of insects buzzing about, along
with the calls of the animals that feed on them. Occasionally, a splash of water
can be heard, as some unseen animal leaves the muddy shore. The air is tinged
with the smell of rotting vegetation and marsh gas, which makes it unpleasant to
breathe.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
Strange insects buzz about, constantly seeking ways to find any exposed flesh.
Their incessant buzzing is punctuated by loud calls of frogs and cries of birds.
The pools of water tend to be shallow and still -- breeding grounds for the
hordes of gnats and midges that give these marshes their name.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
To the north, the tall vertical stalks of reeds form a wall of vegetation. Here,
they are not nearly as dense, and it is possible to see a bit in each direction.
Underfoot, there are about six inches of black mud mixed with rotting vegetation
that grab and suck after each careful footstep.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
This is where the hedge turns south and east, and the hedgetrail follows it
closely. This place is searingly hot on most sunny days, as the hedge deflects
sun back south, and winds slow down among its leaves. The grass that grows here
is dry and brittle at all times, growing to a feets height at most.
name: Bend in the Hedge Trail

desc:
Low grass and tiny clover grow on the sides of the one yard wide hedgetrail that
leads almost all the way to bree to the west and a few miles past weather- top
far east of here. You spot the old east road now and then on the other side of
the hedge to the north, but thorns and thick branches block the way for many
miles.
name: Hedge Trail

desc:
Dry and compassionless ground starts here, swept clean by winds that gain speed
over the barren plains south of here. To your north grows the hedge, and the
hedgetrail quickly crosses this flat platform, leaving east and west.
name: Windy Platform

desc:
You are standing amongst small mounds lacking any vegetation. The hedgetrail
winds east and west among these smooth brown heaps of earth. The northern
horizon is obscured by the hedge which grows thick a few stones away. Desolate
plains are to the south, divided in patches by small trails.
name: A Small Mound

desc:
The old east road lies north of here, and a path leads there through a
wood-beamed archway in the hedge. Many trails lead in various directions over
the plains to the south, the largest being the hedgetrail running east-west here
just south of the hedge.
name: Path to the Old East Road

desc:
Here the path runs southeast and westward, following the hedge closely. Many
small leaves have assembled here in the corner of the hedge where the wind slows
down. The ground is fairly hard and barren though, with only sparse patches of
dirty grass.
name: Southwest of the Hedge

desc:
The ground here is very flat and soft, with lush green-grey grass in all
directions. The air is fresh, carrying the scent of grass. North of here the
plains are somewhat brushy, while the lands to the south and east become less
vegetated. To the west the plains continue.
name: Ancient Battlefields

desc:
The grass has a more brownish tone around here, but is still coarse and fairly
high. Thin young plants of birch and aspen grow here, only a few yards high. The
thin leaves they carry rattle in the wind, creating the sound of many hooves far
away. The brush here continues to the north, while flatlands are to the west and
south. A dark small building has been risen on the plains to the east.
name: Ancient Battlefields

desc:
Thick bushes and tall grass make travelling difficult. To the north the bushes
grow less dense, and high grass offering shelter to all sorts of wild creatures
grows there instead. To the south, the open country seems safer.
name: The Undergrowth

desc:
Bleak, fairly long grass grows here. Here and there you step into holes hidden
beneath the grass, perhaps old graves or traps. These plains stretch out as far
as you can see in all the regions to the south, and also a few miles northwards.
name: Ancient Battlefields

desc:
The grass is mostly brown-red in this area. It is as if the blood that flowed
here and the rust of hundreds of weapons has tainted it for centuries. A large
barren circle where nothing grows is here in the middle of the plains, no doubt
the site of a huge bonfire where corpses were burned.
name: Central Battlefield

desc:
The grass here is tall, it waves along with the wind and makes some whistling
noises. The sky is cloudy and it looks like it could possibly break out into
rain. Though there are patches of blue up there. A few animals can be heard
moving along in the grass. Most likely just some rabbits nothing that would pose
a threat for anyone. However you never know with such tall grass.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The plains are fairly uneven here, with slopes and mounds covered in the very
common grey-green grass that grows all over these vast plains. You notice that a
few mounds has been dug up, as if someone had been searching for something. The
diggings are a few years old though, and grass is growing almost as thick as
before in these places.
name: Ancient Battlefields

desc:
This is an old round Arthedain watchtower. Five yards tall it is still intact a
thousand years after it was raised, except that the stairs that once led up to
the roof has collapsed and lie near the walls on the floor. No furniture remains
here, if there ever has been any. The inside is empty and barren with packed
earth. No arrow slits or windows adorn the walls, and the only light that enters
here is through the door and the hole where the stairs entered the roof.
name: Old Watchtower

desc:
These are the plains just north of a bend in the old east road. While grey-green
grass covers most of the plains north and west of here, the landscape to the
east is barren, and devoid of any visible wildlife. A few overgrown brooks are
barely visible beneath the thick grass; they are only about a foot deep and
seldom filled with water.
name: Plains

desc:
For a few miles south and east are dead, barren plains, and the land here is
also devoid of most vegetation. Dry, cracked earth with small tufts of grass
cover this area. You notice that the mounds that cover this area very often are
giant anthills, some of them over three yards in diameter. To the west and
northwest the grassy plains continue.
name: Plains

desc:
This is a few miles of barren land where nothing grows and nothing moves. Even
the coarse grey grass that is so common in this region refuses to grow here. To
the north and east the plains are more fertile and low scrubs are scattered
about the land, while this desolate landscape continues eastward. South and
west, the road makes a turn just in front of the Great Hedge.
name: Dead Plains

desc:
The land here is flat and rather dry, with large patches where nothing cares to
grow. Mostly though, greyish grass cover the ground. To the north and west, the
grass grows thicker and more colourful. On the plains west of here stands an old
watchtower, with the door looking this way. South and east of here lie barren
lands.
name: Plains

desc:
Flat plains with little else than grass growing upon them continue in all
quarters from here. Slowly rolling past old battlefields to the northern edge of
the plains, south towards the old east road, and west towards the midgewaters,
these plains cover an area of about a fifty square leagues. Not much seems to be
moving upon them, but you can spot birds of prey in the sky, perhaps hunting
rabbits and fieldmice that might live in the vicinity.
name: Plains

desc:
The old east road runs south of here, and the thick hedge follows it close on
the south side. This is on the edge of vast grasscovered plains that stretch out
in all directions on the north side of the road. A carcass surrounded by flies
and inhabited by worms lies rotting here, deceased a few days ago. The stench
can be felt a hundred yards away to the sensitive nose.
name: Plains

desc:
Coarse grey-green grass grows in all directions here. Once the battlefields
where the armies of Cardolan and Arthedain armies fought off the witchkings'
blood must have flowed abundantly here. Small, shy blue flowers grow plentiful
here, a strong contrast to the history of these plains.
name: Ancient Battlefields

desc:
Dullish grey-green grass grow here on very white soil. Perhaps so many bones
have been crushed here that the ground has changed colour. Plains stretch far
away in all directions.
name: Ancient Battlefields

desc:
Low bushes with black stems and fat green leaves grow sparsely here in the grey
short grass. This is the northern reach of the battleplains, as the vegetation
grows too dense to the north for any such activities. In all other directions
these grass covered plains continue though.
name: Ancient Battlefields

desc:
These plains are full of simple grass, a good resting place for your animals.
The sun is beautiful here and the winds make the grass dance along the ground. A
few rabbits scurry out of sight and some other squirrels roam around by their
trees. The ground is nice and flat, easing your feet as you walk. The clouds fly
by dancing in the blue skies.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
There is nothing but tall grass as far as you can see. Clouds make shadows along
the ground as they are whisked around by the wind. Rabbits hop around here
gnawing on the grass as you walk by. This is a very calm and peaceful place. The
tranquility of the rustling grass and clear blue sky calls to you like the homes
of your childhood.
name: Long, Tall Grass

desc:
Here is another patch of shrubs along the trail. This part of the land though
does not look like it is meant for traveling upon. Though there are quite a few
berries along here, there isn't much in the way of anything else around here. It
may be just a good idea to head back up towards the trail.
name: A Patch along the Trail

desc:
Here is a little patch of land near a trail. The ground here is covered with
weeds and it feels like some of them maybe making you to itch. This is not a
pleasant place to spend any time. The trail looks more well tended and that
would be a better place to spend your travels.
name: A Field near a Trail

desc:
The river makes its way along and you feel a breeze going up your back. The
water is a murky-looking green, and even though this is a rather small river,
you cannot really see the bottom of it. The currents change very suddenly, maybe
you could catch some bass or something. There is for sure something in this
river.
name: Drifting in the Green River

desc:
Insects buzz around your head as you slosh your way through the thick mud. The
putrid smell of decomposition assaults your nostrils as you make your way
around. Swatting the annoying insects away from your eyes, you cannot see much
of anything else living here. Only a few dead trees break the monotony of the
brown slush beneath your feet.
name: Wet Plains near a River

desc:
This is a small pond where whoever tends these fields most likely gets his water
from. It looks quite safe to drink and from what you have seen of the farmer's
work that is probably true.
name: A Small Pond

desc:
You have entered a fenced in yard of some sort. From the looks of it, this seems
to be part of a farm. To whom it might belong you are not at all certain. The
grass is thick and lush here and, in the odd patches of sod, you detect tracks
that could be from horses or cows. Perhaps this is an animal pen.
name: A Small Yard

desc:
Half buried in the dirt, you notice bits of brightly colored wood that seem to
resemble childrens' toys. The ground is flat and the grass smoothed over, as if
trampled by many feet. In fact, as you listen, you can hear laughter coming from
the north. Stumbling over the toys, you are able to make your way through this
field.
name: A Little Field

desc:
You have entered the humble abode of a small farmer's family. The children are
all out running around playing and the farmer can be found around here somewhere
tending after his farm. The hut is a small building made of logs from the
forests up north. It seems to be sturdy and the family seems to be a friendly
one.
name: Farmer's Hut

desc:
Every where you look you see nothing but bushes and wild grass. Insects fly
about, dancing and playing. The flowers smell wonderful as you tread on through
the tall grass. The sounds in the air are quiet and peaceful. It is nice except
for all the insects biting your arms and legs.
name: Small Plains

desc:
Here is just a little grassy hideaway, a little pond is down in the centre. The
sun reflects off the water. Animals can be heard relaxing in this nice cool
spot. The flowers around here are blooming and beautiful. The land is covered in
lush green grass. This is one spot that you could just relax and sleep in
forever.
name: A Grassy Notch

desc:
The flowers in this patch are all multicolored and smell a wonderful variety of
smells. The patch is natural but has a well kept look to it. Running through
here and just frolicking in the flowers sounds actually like a nice vacation
from your long adventure.
name: A Small Flower Patch

desc:
There is nothing but tall grass as far as you can see, clouds make shadows along
the ground as they are whisked around by the wind. There are rabbits moving
around here gnawing on the grass as you walk by. This is a very calm peaceful
place. The rustling of the grass along with the blue sky makes it a place that
you would want to make a home around here.
name: Quiet Plains

desc:
A large Oak tree is here old and looking almost dead. It looks to be a fort for
some children but they aren't around right now. The tree is large and black and
roots are sticking out of the ground every where. Some flowers grow by the
bottom of the tree and little grass near it as well. Very few animals seem to be
moving around here.
name: An Oak Tree

desc:
The plains around here seem to go on for ever and you could easily get lost in
them. All the land you seem able to see is that of tall grass. The few trees in
this area all look alike. A feeling of boredom strikes you as you look out over
this plain looking landscape.
name: A Lost Plain

desc:
The grass here is tall, it waves along with the wind and makes some whistling
noises. The sky is cloudy and it looks like it could possibly break out into
rain. Though there are patches of blue up there. A few animals can be heard
moving along in the grass. Most likely just some rabbits nothing that would pose
a threat for anyone. However you never know with such tall grass.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The grass here is tall, it waves along with the wind and makes some whistling
noises. The sky is cloudy and it looks like it could possibly break out into
rain. Though there are patches of blue up there. A few animals can be heard
moving along in the grass. Most likely just some rabbits nothing that would pose
a threat for anyone. However you never know with such tall grass.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The trees around here seem to be littered with bird's nests. This looks to be a
form of sanctuary with them. They eye you closely as you enter their area. You
can see all different kinds of birds here. Many of them are flying off. Feathers
are floating everywhere and the birds' screeches are the only thing you are able
to hear.
name: A Nesting Place

desc:
There is nothing but tall grass as far as you can see, clouds make shadows along
the ground as they are whisked around by the wind. There are rabbits moving
around here gnawing on the grass as you walk by. This is a very calm peaceful
place. The rustling of the grass along with the blue sky makes it a place that
you would want to make a home around here.
name: Quiet Plains

desc:
There is nothing but tall grass as far as you can see; clouds make shadows along
the ground as they are whisked around by the wind. There are rabbits moving
around here nibbling on the grass as you walk by. This is a very calm and
peaceful place.
name: A Quiet Prairie

desc:
You lie on a small hill, the grass is nice and green to the touch. The wind
blows through the nearby trees. A squirrel moves close to you as you have
invaded it's tree. The branches of the tree make creaking noises as the wind
moves through them. The grass looks like an ocean with each wave another breath
of the wind. The sun is setting over the hill casting red and orange rays
dancing into the sky.
name: A Resting Place

desc:
This is the room where the inhabitants of this mansion cook and sleep. While the
iron bars in the old fireplace have been recently replaced, the beds are
rudimentary and dirty. On the floor someone has drawn large intersecting circles
in different colours and a mirror of polished steel is located next to the exit.
Other than these features the room is unadorned.
name: Left Wing of a Dunadan Mansion

desc:
Low, dry and brittle grey-green grass covers this tableland. A few white flowers
are hiding in the grass, sometimes forming small patches. The ground drops
fastly off to the north, while it remains flat and easily travelled in the other
directions.
name: Plateau on the Plains

desc:
Low grey grass covers this plateau. While the ground inclines too sharply to be
passable to the north, you could probably make it down to the marshes west of
here. In the summer grasshoppers chirp in the grass, while during winter the
occasional hole in the snow indicates some kind of subterranean animal. The
plateau stretches out southeast for a few miles, flat and featureless all the
way to the edges.
name: Plateau on the Plains

desc:
This is just above the easternmost regions of Midgewater. You can see the swamps
down below a sheer drop down to the west. You are about twenty yards up, and
swallows circle at about this height catching mosquitoes over the murky waters.
The grass grows thick here, and the plateau is green and flat for a long
distance in all directions but the west.
name: Plateau on the Plains

desc:
Very flat and featureless the plateau continues in all directions from here.
Sparse bushes try to survive, begging for rain during hot summers. They have
thin branches and seldom carry anything else than bitter, vaguely poisonous
fruits.
name: Plateau on the Plains

desc:
This is the eastern part of a very flat plateau, all covered in green-grey
grass. Looking east, you see a few abandoned structures down on lower plains,
and beyond that the ground rises towards the weather hills. Many bushes grow in
an area near this eastern edge, which is very steep, and they intertwine in such
a way as to make them impossible to penetrate.
name: Plateau on the Plains

desc:
Thick brush covers the northern part of this plateau, and there is little hope
of getting through them. You notice uprooted grass along a wide streak here, as
if large animals had passed by in a horde lately. The ground slopes slowly down
to the east.
name: Plateau on the Plains

desc:
This is the southeastern peak of the plateau, rising to a height of about thirty
yards above the plains to the south and east. A watchtower in wood has been
raised here long ago, in order to overlook these plains. Nowadays nothing more
than the basic structure is left standing, and the floor and roof lie in a
rotting heap on the ground. You can see the thin line of the Old East Road far
south and the hedge rising just beyond it. The ground slopes slowly downwards in
that direction, while the cliff is far too steep to be climbable to the east.
name: Plateau on the Plains

desc:
A small brook starts here and leads down the southern side, carrying off some
water down to the lower plains when it has rained heavily. Some heather grows
nearby. The slope to the south should not be too hard to manage, even if it
might take a while.
name: Plateau on the Plains

desc:
The plateau has a very unsecure and steep slope down its western side. A few low
pine trees grow on the edge. They have a hard time clinging on to the loose
soil, and their roots are bared as rain erodes the plateau and washes it down
into the marshes below. You notice small stubs hidden in the grass, so probably
quite a forest grew here once. The plateau dips down to the south also, but less
dramatic. Down there are wet bushy plains.
name: Plateau on the Plains

desc:
This is probably the easternmost tongue of Midgewater. Lots of bushes and ferns
grow on these soggy plains. There is no clear water here though, only the
occasional feet-deep mudhole. You hear the quaking of frogs all around, and this
is probably a perfect breedingplace for them. A sloping bank up to the north
will take you to dryer grounds.
name: Soggy Plains

desc:
The tableland to the north has left quite a few large stones here at its base,
as the earth has been eroded and washed away out on the plains during the
centuries. Among them live many ferns, and for some reason there are moss-
covered logs here also.
name: Plains

desc:
You are amidst fairly thick and high grey grass which beckons in the wind. These
plains slowly ascend a tableland to the north, and turn into bushland to the
west. South of here is more of this rich grass, and beyond this you can outline
the hedge following the whole southern horizon.
name: Plains

desc:
You are on flat, grassy plains below the southeast corner of a plateau. This
feaureless landscape continues to the east. West of here, where this tableland
slopes down and enters the plains, they grow bushier and more vegetated. No
signs of humans ever living in the near vicinity can be seen, but a few miles to
the north you can outline the silhouette of a structure of sorts. The ground
gets rougher to the south, more wrinkled with dips and small hills.
name: Plains

desc:
The old ruin of a house stands here on the middle of these empty plains.
Although the building probably is no more than a few hundred years old and
therefore was built after the wars, it must have seen its share of misery. Any
inhabitants are long gone, and the estate is in a state of permanent decay. The
fields surrounding it grow weeds, the well is filled with soil, and the
outhouses are probably burnt down. The only thing that remains and witnesses of
a once very well-tended farm is the roof. Still supported by ten thick wooden
beams, it defies storms, snow and heat. Covered with thick grass, it is still in
such a good shape as to keep the ground below dry under even heavy rainfalls.
During winter, though, snow will blow in through the sides and cover the rotting
walls and the broken jars and pots that still lie decaying on the floor. A fence
once surrounded this place, but now only a few poles remain standing a few
hundred yards out on the plains.
name: Ruin with a Roof

desc:
Bushes grow close to the sheer cliff of the plateau to the west here. Some cling
as well to the cliffside that rises fifteen yards or more. A ravine seems to
lead west, well hidden by these bushes. The entrance is only about two yards
wide, and hard to spot. East, north and south are plains, and a mile or so south
the skeleton of a building can be seen.
name: Plains

desc:
The flats you are on stretch out seemingly endlessly towards the south and the
east, while the plains slope gradually upwards to the west, and forms a plateau.
The ground crepitates as you move, and you notice there are many old branches
and twigs hidden in the thick grass. You pass many stumps, so this once must
have been quite a forest.
name: Stumps on the Plains

desc:
You have entered a rather harsh patch of shrubs, the shrubs are really beginning
to tear at your clothes. This is a rather difficult area to move through.
However it doesn't seem to last very long and nice land is in sight not far
away.
name: A Patch of Shrubs

desc:
There are some wild blackberries growing around here. The shrubs are wooded and
covered in small pricklers that make picking their fruit hard to do. However
there look to be some really ripe blue and black berries here along with a few
other type of berries.
name: A Black Berry Patch

desc:
The grass here is long and tall. Little trees dot the area forming a kind of a
path. Underneath the trees, small plants, perhaps ferns, grow, forming a sort of
hedge. The trees are small and just provide enough presence to unnerve you. The
animals around here are small and they tend to stay hidden from you.
name: A Long, Grassy Patch

desc:
On these plains, where once fierce battle stood between Arthedain forces and the
hordes of the witchking, only small, barely visible mounds recognizable as
graves witness of the bloodshed. The air seems thick and hard to breath however
hard the wind blows. The spirits of the deceased perhaps feel disturbed by your
presence.
name: Ancient Battlefields

desc:
Grey-green grass covers these ancient battlegrounds. Some old stones mark the
corners of an old house that once must have stood here. Living must have been
poor, as armies probably ransacked this homestead on every passing by. Some
clover still grows in the old fields where cattle once were kept. The only sign
of a well is the ring of stones a few yards south of the building.
name: Ancient Battlefields

desc:
These are the outskirts of the old battlefields. Thick, coarse grey-green grass
cover the ground, and you trample in a hidden mound full of rusted metalpieces.
The plain continues in all directions, with not much moving upon it.
name: Ancient Battlefields

desc:
The grass is fairly heavy to walk in, as it is about two feet tall around here.
The land is flat in all directions, and you notice very little around here that
moves across the land, except for a few rabbit ears sticking up in the grass now
and then.
name: Plains

desc:
This is flat land with grey-green grass, stretching out in all quarters except
the southwest where the old east road runs past some ashen trees. Some kind of
campsite has been set up there next to the road. You travel easily here, almost
bouncing along, as the ground is soft and spongy.
name: Plains

desc:
South of here the road goes in east- and westwardly directions. West is a bushy
area with many small depressions, while the endless plains you are on continue
to the north and east. The only thing that really stands out around here are a
few higher bushsprouts, boldly declaring their right to live here in the thick
grass.
name: Plains

desc:
You are among the small dips north of the old east road. Bushes with brittle
grey leaves grow in plentitude here. Tiny red and yellow flowers compete with
the grass and the bushes about shadow in the deeper parts of the depressions.
Although this is one of the lower parts of the plains you can find no water here
except after a rain, when the ground becomes soggy.
name: Plains

desc:
Tall, rich grass grows upon this soft ground. Walking is strenuous while at
foot. Many bushes grow near the road south of here, with thick, moist leaves
that will glisten on sunny days.
name: Plains

desc:
A few small groves grow far apart here on the plains, mainly birch and alder
trees.You have found one with a pool. Ten feet down it lies, with few fatbladed
flowers floating on the surface. As the banks leading down to the pond are very
slippery, you have to approach it with care so you do not fall into the water.
name: Plains

desc:
These plains north of the old east road is fairly bushy, with moist ground.
Someone has tried to dig a ditch, either to feed the plains with water or drain
them, but the project must have been abandoned and the ditch is only about a
mile or so. The road runs just south of here, making a bend towards the east and
south.
name: Moist Plains

desc:
Grey and featureless plains seem to roll on forever here. No signs of water can
be seen, and the air trembles from heat on hot days. In wintertime these plains
are just as ruthless, with nowhere to hide from the biting frosty winds.
name: Plains

desc:
This is far out on the plains, with little civilization in sight. A house can be
seen far away to the west, but that is all. Grey-green coarse grass covers the
land, rolling in the wind like waves at sea. A long way from here on the
northeastern horizon you can discern the dark outlining of the Weather Hills. On
the northern horizon lie more mountains, beyond what looks like a forest. South
is the old east road and the hedge, while behind a plateau to the west lurk the
dark and devious Midgewater marshes.
name: Plains

desc:
Here lies the stone foundation of a keep or mansion of some sort. Complete with
kitchen, stables, yard, and a diversity of rooms and halls this must have been
the home of some quite prominent person. Unless the stones have been carried for
use elsewhere, this building must used wood for walls and ceilings. No objects
will be found here, as the place must have been abandoned about a thousand years
ago, around the time of the witchking wars. Also you see no signs of any cellar
or subterranean construction, except for the well which is filled with earth and
overgrown with grass. A mountain ash grows where there once were household
fields, carrying a few dark red fruits for any hungry birds.
name: Plains

desc:
Once a probably very beautiful deciduous forest must have grown here as the land
is flat and open. Stubs and a lot of old twigs and branches are hidden in the
grass among the bushes. Grey grass covered plains stretch out towards the south
and east, while the ground gets higher a few miles to the west. North are thick
bushes, and you cannot hope to come far in that direction.
name: Stumps on the Plains

desc:
The river has gotten rather shallow and the edges of the bank are rather muddy.
To the west there looks to be a pond that you can possibly go into. The river is
a really murky dark green. Highly unlikely anything lives in this area. You
would be better off just leaving this area and heading on with your adventure.
Heading towards the pond might be a good idea. To the east you can enter the
river much better. The river seems to get a little wider as you go east.
name: On a Small River

desc:
The River seems to be widening alittle. It is rather murky and muddy. Moving is
not an easy feat to do with the current so slow. You find that putting your feet
in here would not be a pleasant experience. It is best just to keep moving and
find someplace to get off this river. The breeze in the air is rather chilling.
The sun is heading off, this is not a good place to be spending a night. Better
that you keep moving.
name: In the Small River

desc:
The river is now wide and fairly slow, it is probably best to just let your
craft float if you are headed down river, and just sit back and relax. A few
bass can be seen swimming along in the river, and something else as well though
the movements are hard to tell what they are, they look like twigs off a fallen
log. Timber lays around the shore line, that is most likely the case.
name: Along the Green River

desc:
You are in the Green River. The river for some strange reason, has a green color
to it, maybe this is why they named it the Green River? It is also cool and the
bottom is too cloudy to see. Something greenish seems to move along the bottom
of the river. You might be curious but you are not that curious to see what is
down there. The breeze keeps you floating and daydreaming of past adventures.
Best just to keep daydreaming and float on down the river.
name: In the Green River

desc:
You are rafting through a nice calm river. Moving circles in the calm water with
your hands, you slowly drift into thought. You could just let the raft take you
anywhere it leads. The current seems to have some irregular movement in it
though. The water is a murky greenish color and a few logs float along in here
with you. Some insects can be seen dancing along the water, skimming from log to
log.
name: The Green River

desc:
The river is rather murky. You can not see the bottom of it even though you know
it isn't all that deep. The sand in the river seems to be drifting about as if
there is something moving along the bottom. Here it looks as if you could take a
dive down, to see what it is that is under the water. The mud though looks
rather bad near the bottom and the water is all too quiet to want to dive down
there. It is probably best just to go on and forget about diving in here.
