name: A Murky Green River

desc:
The woods along the bank can be seen quite well from here. You notice that the
water is getting darker as you head down river. The currents are erractic and
they don't seem to ever move in a normal pattern. The insects along the side of
the river move slightly along the top of the waters. The water moves ever so
slow. Best that you keep yourself alert, you never know what might be lurking in
the waters below....
name: Green Forest River

desc:
This area is darkened by the tall trees along the bank of the river. The cool
breeze chills you. You can feel the waters coolness on your ankles when you dip
your feet in. You could go for a swim in the clear green waters of the river.
However, the moving of shapes in the water suggest that it would not be such a
smart idea here to do.
name: Wooded Forest River

desc:
Surrounded by fifteen yards sheer rockwalls with only a two yards wide ravine
leading out to the plains east of here, this valley is well hidden. Thick brush
grows on the rim, hiding it also from detection from above. Pine trees cover
most of the hundred yards wide space, while a large wooden mansion lies below
the north valleyside. A faint path leads through the trees up to the stairs and
the porch. The house lies silent like a large resting animal, hungrily awaiting
foolhardy intruders to force its half-rotten frontdoor and go astray within its
leaky wooden walls.
name: Hidden Valley

desc:
Two red carpets lie across the blankpolished black floor in this large hallroom.
The ceiling is white, with two unlit large lustres hanging in heavy chains.
Straight ahead a large stairway leads up to the second floor, made of dark brown
wood with sturdy banisters, and one of the carpets lead from the front door up
the stairs. The other carpet runs between a doubledoor in the west wall and
another more plain door in the east. Four lamps are attached to the wall, which
also is adorned with masks and garments. A small bell is protruding from the
wall beside a large curtain on the left side of the stairs, and just to the
right of the frontdoor stands a small pulpet with an open book, a quill, and an
inkjug.
name: A Noble's House

desc:
Parquet floor covers this room, worn out between a brown drape in the north wall
and the large table in the middle of the room. A white and brown wooly carpet is
spread below the table, and one large seat is at each end. A fireplace is in the
northeast corner, and fresh flowers spread a sweet scent throughout the room
from their place in a vase in front of it. In the ceiling which is crisscrossed
by many thick fillets of chestnut is an unlit lustre. Almost the whole southern
wall is covered by a huge painting. Everything is in very good shape, and
meticulously attended.
name: Dining Room

desc:
A forest shaded in by heavy trees blackening out the sunlight, creatures must
use this area for an entrance to the river to get something to drink. A murky
green river lurks towards the east from here. The shadows dance along the trees
from your lantern, music can seem to be heard off in the distance. This very
well could have been an ancient burial ground, the strangness of the land along
with that cool breeze from the river, sends chills down your spine.
name: The Dark Woods

desc:
The trees whistle with the wind, rustling can be heard all around you. This is
still a dark and mysterious area. The trees are huge and move to block out all
possibility of light. There are scurrying sounds all around you, looking about
though you can not see anything. The ground is damp and is mucking at the feet.
As far as you can see there are nothing but trees to look at.
name: A Black Forest

desc:
Claw marks litter the trees along here, this is some predator's territory and it
seems you may have entered it. Exits out of here are difficult as that the trees
blacken out any light from above. The soil is moist and soft to the touch making
stepping cool and the breeze chills your body.
name: A Dark Black Forest

desc:
Here are the woods blackened by the high limbs of age old trees, this land is
full of noises and night predators can be heard devouring their little day prey.
This is not a good place to set up a camp. A few old relics seem to lie
scattered about, as if a hunting party had been through here.
name: The Black Forest

desc:
The woods here shade you from the sunlight above. It is hard to tell if there is
even any daylight. Night animals roam around here freely preying on poor
travellers who are not well versed in forest life. Movements seem to be coming
and going all around you. This does not seem like a very safe place to stay too
long. Sounds of crunching can be heard all over the forest. The darkness and the
dense trees make it hard to tell which way you should go.
name: The Dark Woods

desc:
Along here there comes the sounds of a slow flowing river nearby. The woods are
closed in by many trees blocking out the sunlight. The wind moves through the
trees making whining sounds that seem to cover up a more real noise. The ground
being soft and moist makes this not a comfortable place to rest.
name: Some Woods near a River

desc:
Along here the soil is soggy and the trees are as black as the land itself. The
woods are full of animals moving about, as you turn around quickly you see a
small wolf devour a rabbit. The land is dominated by the night creatures. The
river can be heard not too far away. There you will find something to drink and
a place to rest.
name: A Murky Forest along the River

desc:
Along here you can hear the gently moving waters, the ground before you is rough
and hard to move along. The night give way to night predators, however, you
realise that it is still day time. The trees are so close and huge that they
black out any possibilities of seeing the sun. Night animals lurk around but
every where you turn you see nothing but trees.
name: A Dark Forest along the River

desc:
As you look ahead to what you have just entered. You see many little hills. The
possibility of Hobbits living here is a good one. Though there hasn't been any
really seen yet. There could be still some in among those hills. As you know the
hobbits like to live in the hills. This could be a good place to gain some more
supplies. The hills are sloped gently and travel is easy on them. The grass is
soft and green and walking here you can just relax and daydream as you move
along.
name: In among the Hills

desc:
This hill is much higher than the rest of them and you can see for miles. A few
trees cover the hills and down toward the south one giant tree seems to have
provided shelter for old travlers and new adventurers. There could be a good
place to rest. Here though is nothing but grass, while the flowers are pretty to
look at, your attention can't be kept here for very long.
name: Over the Hills

desc:
This trail looks like very familiar. You seem to be heading towards what sounds
like a river. Toward the east there looks to be a possible river. You could head
back north, the trail near the hills is smooth and without much dangers. It
seems to be a well traveled trail. With adventurers on it often going to and
from many different parts of the world. You could take the somewhat old looking
path that leads along the flatlands to the west. Towards the east there seems to
be an old path leading down toward the river.
name: A Trail along the Hills

desc:
Old armour lay strewn along this trail. Bones of dead warriors are scattered
around. The charcoal remains of some of them can be seen near burnt ground. Once
great swords lay broken in the rubble. The victors seem to have made a trail
onward, though this one seems to have been laid to rest, an ancient burial
ground. Along the trail lies a breast plate with an inscription.
name: A Lost Trail

desc:
This hill is more of just a steep incline than a hill. The grass on here is a
luscious green and the flowers are blooming. A few bees fly around here
collecting nectar. The sound of birds can be heard playing amongst the tree.
This is a lovely place for a rest. All around you you can see only more gentle
hills and trees for shade.
name: A Small Hill

desc:
A large tree stands here, of a size which can only be appreciated by walking all
round its large base. Its wide canopy would surely provide some shelter from
foul weather, or a welcome shade, on a hot sunny day, and the grass, which
grows, thick and lush, among its tangled roots, would make a pleasant place to
sit, or even take a nap.
name: A Large Tree on Top of a Hill

desc:
At the top of this hill you see nothing but more gentle hills, for some weary
hobbits this could make a lovely place for a home. Around here is nothing but
blue skies with few white clouds whisking around in the breeze. It is a place
that is both relaxing and nice to explore, who knows what might be out there
among the different hills.
name: A Small Hill

desc:
This is a dark and shady place, there is almost no vegetation and everything
looks cold and grey. Dry mud is cowering the ground and only a little grass
manages to grow. There is no wind and the air is thick and old.
name: Shaded Nook

desc:
You have hit the river. Fish seem to be swimming by and if you step out east you
will be swimming in a nice cool calm river. However it would be best to get a
raft and try to boat across cause there looks to be some things that may not be
quite so nice also swimming along in the river with you.
name: Down along the Green River

desc:
You find yourself floating along the river. Its murky green water is cool to the
touch. A few old logs lie along the bank. Dark little shifts move through the
water. Most likely just some bass swimming along. The sun slowly heads down and
you lie here and just float along and think of times back at your house when you
were a kid. Ripples rock the raft slightly and a cool breeze can be felt against
the face.
name: Floating through the Green River

desc:
Looking up you can see the bridge you had thought about crossing. Now it may
have been safe but you really don't like taking chances. As you go under it you
look up and see some fellow travellers heading across it. Well maybe it was safe
after all. The bank of the river is too steep to really get out here to go check
the safety of the bridge. Why not just keep moving along. You are bound to find
an exit somewhere off this river.
name: Beneath a Rope Bridge

desc:
This cleverly concealed trail has been marked with pieces of dark cloth tied
among the bushes at regular intervals. It weaves its way among the fens, heading
generally east and west. The path would be almost impossible to spot if one did
not know exactly where to look.
name: Secret Trail

desc:
Winding amongst the fens, this tortuous trail skilfully takes advantage of the
exposed land to lead generally east and west. Small pieces of dark cloth tied at
regular intervals to bushes mark its course, but it would be almost impossible
to find if one did not know exactly where to look.
name: Secret Trail

desc:
Here lay more bones of gallant warriors, the grounds around them seem burned as
well, a few large scales lay across the land. Elven daggers lie amongst orkish
armour. The skulls of obviously northern Orcs lay in large holes along the
ground. The battle seems to have continued on for quite some time. Another plate
is inscribed with an insignia.
name: A Lost Trail

desc:
Fewer bones lay along this trail, it appears you may be reaching the horror that
these poor souls could not avoid. Daggers seem to mark the road as if they had
been searching for a way out of this terrible passage. Spears lay along the
ground embedded in some scales, the ground is hot and dry, no grass grows here
still. Battle cries can be heard as the wind picks up from where the battle once
raged. A dagger with battle scars lies in the middle of the trail. Along the
blade is a small warning.
name: A Lost Trail

desc:
A large final battle has seemed to have taken place, all around you you see
hundreds of decaying bones and scarred armour. This is truly the end of the
trail, the predator here has shown its cunning and intelligence. Many of your
own brethren have died to pay you forbidance. You can hear stirring from the
north. The trail seems to just blacken as you look forward into nothingness. A
skeleton stands here in full warrior armour with one hand reaching up at you.
Again one final message is left on his breastplate.
name: The Lost Trail

desc:
All around you spreads a blackened and burnt field. No smoke rises from the
ground, as the damage was done long ago. Still, you see the charred bones and
armour scattered on the ground, and no trees or vegetation grow here.
name: A Scorched Trail

desc:
Hidden in a desolate valley surrounded by almost impassable shrubs, this house
might have evaded attention for centuries. Lately someone has begun the
refurnishment of the exterior by patching the roof and covering the walls with
black sticky tar in hope that the wooden planks can withstand another decade.
The interior appears to consist of three fairly large rooms, with this one being
the largest. A few plush armchairs and sofas provide comfort; while a large dark
table, which is surrounded by oaken chairs, probably serves equally as dining
table and meeting table judging by the number of notes and diagrams spread
across it.
name: Refurnished Dunadan Mansion

desc:
The walls and windows of this room are covered completely with red and black
sheets. Several shields and stuffed animal heads protrude in the places the
sheets join with each other. A steel lamp, constructed as a spiral, is hanging
from the ceiling and focuses its beam on the centre of the room where a strange
book is attached with iron bolts to a heavy dark oak pedestal.
name: Right Wing of a Dunadan Mansion

desc:
It is difficult to walk here, as the thick black mud underfoot grabs and holds
feet, and the reeds -- some more than an inch in diameter -- press close on
every side. To the north, the stand of reeds becomes even more tangled, while in
every other direction they gradually peter out into the standard vegetation of
the marsh.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
The frequent stretches of deep mud and black ponds makes it impossible to travel
in a given direction with any certainty. On all sides the marshes reign
unchallenged, providing a home to ravenous hordes of biting and stinging
insects, who in turn sustain a huge population of frogs and other less familiar
predators.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:

in a given direction with any certainty. On all sides the marshes reign
unchallenged, providing a home to ravenous hordes of biting and stinging
insects, who in turn sustain a huge population of frogs and other less familiar
predators.
name: Midgewater Marshes

insects, who in turn sustain a huge population of frogs and other less familiar
predators.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
The reeds in this area are dense almost beyond belief. Each step requires a huge
effort to clear a path, and after passage the resilient reeds spring back as if
no one had been there at all.
name: Dense Reeds

desc:
The reeds press in on all sides, obscuring vision in every direction. The ground
is covered with a foot of black water, and below the water, thick mud sucks at
each footstep. Occasionally the incessant hum of the insects is broken by the
splash of something falling into the water.
name: Dense Reeds

desc:
The soil in this dense stand of sedges is very moist, but not so much that it
sticks to boots. All around the buzzing of hordes of insects fills the air. To
the south a thick blanket of mist covers the ground, but in every other
direction the marsh continues.
name: Sedges

desc:
This is a dense stand of sedges mixed with various species of marsh plants. The
noise of multitudes of insects is omnipresent -- a constant reminder of why this
place was called the Midgewater.
name: Sedges

desc:
In every direction, the marshes continue unchallenged. It is nearly impos- sible
to move consistently in a given direction, as every few feet some obstacle or
another presents itself. Here the primary obstruction are dense impassable
growths of sedges that cluster together on may small islets.
name: Sedges

desc:
Much of the open water here is covered with green algae that hides the murky
depths beneath. The odour of marsh gas and rotten vegetation is prominent, and
the smell seems to cling to everything. The black mud has the consistency of
thick soup, and care must be taken not to step into the sporadic deep pockets of
it.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
Deep pockets of mud and frequent stretches of murky water make travel here an
arduous ordeal. Each step must be taken with care, for who knows how far one may
sink into the muck. The incessant droning of throngs of insects cannot drown out
the occasional splash of some larger creature taking to the water.
name: Midgewater Marshes

in a given direction with any certainty. On all sides the marshes reign
unchallenged, providing a home to ravenous hordes of biting and stinging
insects, who in turn sustain a huge population of frogs and other less familiar
predators.
name: Midgewater Marshes

insects, who in turn sustain a huge population of frogs and other less familiar
predators.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
To the north, the dense tangles of chaotic vegetation are not as frequent. Here,
each movement is a struggle against the green tendrils of plant life that always
seem to be thickest in the direction one travels. Many frogs and less familiar
amphibians have grown fat and bloated on the abundant insect life, and their
expressionless eyes can be seen everywhere.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
Travel through this area forces a difficult choice on the wanderer -- whether to
slog through the murky water or to fight through the tangled knots of sedges
that completely cover every inch of exposed land. The hordes of insects that
fill the air gleefully dive at every bit of skin left uncovered, inflicting
annoying bites and stings.
name: Sedges

desc:

in a given direction with any certainty. On all sides the marshes reign
unchallenged, providing a home to ravenous hordes of biting and stinging
insects, who in turn sustain a huge population of frogs and other less familiar
predators.

insects, who in turn sustain a huge population of frogs and other less familiar
predators.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
Here looks to be many large holes in the hill. Probably just an old Hobbit
dwelling. Looking around most of them do not lead anywhere but back a few feet
then end abruptly. Looking around though is leading to a few that aren't so
deadended. A couple of the holes look like they could go down further than just
a couple feet.
name: An Opening through the Hill

desc:
Walking along here you go over another little hill. This land is just full of
hills and nothing else. Some trees grow around here providing shade for that
summer heat or protection from rain and snow. There are a couple of holes in the
hills most likely just some moles or something. Still, exploring can be a lot of
fun in an area of little danger like this.
name: Trail around the Hills

desc:
The Great Hedge blocks access to the Old East Road which runs on the other side
just to the north. This trail is seldom-used as there are very few breaks in the
hedge to allow access. Where the grass is still worn away along the trail, the
tracks of many sheep are evident.
name: Trail along the Hedge

desc:
This seldom-used trail mirrors the Old East Road that runs alongside the
opposite side of the Great Hedge to the north. In many places, the trail is
almost lost due to the encroachment of the rich grass that covers the gentle
hills of the South Downs, which continue to the southern horizon.
name: Trail along the Hedge

desc:
The Great Hedge bends from west to south here, and the trail is forced to bend
with it. This portion of the trail is becoming overgrown with the grasses common
on the downs to the south, and sometimes it is lost for a bit under the carpet
of grass.
name: Trail along the Hedge

desc:
You notice that you can barely travel along this path. You look down and you see
that there maybe a path leading down to the river. You could head back toward
the trail, or you could head on along the river. There are a lot of bushes and
small trees that wandering too far around could give you some unwanted troubles.
The river looks like it could be a nice place to take a swim. You can go south
back to the trail or lead on north along the bank. Maybe you can even go down
the slope down to the river.
name: A Trail near the River

desc:
This trail seems to have been deserted a long time ago. It is more just a path
with little grass growing on it around the hill. Along the hill is a lot of lush
grass growing, a good place to rest any horses and allow them to feed. The wind
blows cold around the hill and all around you you can only see more hills. There
seems to be no civilization out there.
name: An Overgrown Trail around the Hills

desc:
This forest is lovely, with lots of fresh air to breathe and beautiful things to
behold. The trees are huge and spaced apart in a maze like way. Squirrels and
other little animals populate the trees. Best be careful though, other animals
live in these kind of areas and they aren't as cute as squirrels. The ground is
soft and moist as if a river is nearby feeding the woodland grounds.
name: Woodland Area

desc:
You have entered a yard that is fenced in. Obviously you have found someone's
farm. Who they are though is another question. There are some horses here. along
with a few other animals. The yard has a lot of grass for the animals and it
looks to be well kept as well.
name: A Small Yard

desc:
The river seems really green and dark. You can barely see what looks like the
bottom. The mud on the bottom of the river seems to stir every time you paddle
why could that be? You can see what looks like a forest up above the bank of the
river. You should keep on moving and keep alert. This does not look like a good
place to be caught sleeping.
name: On a Green River

desc:
Tall grasses and short woody-stemmed plants inhabit the spaces between the large
boulders that mar the steep hillsides sweeping upward, away from this gentle
valley. Birds and small animals can be observed among the brush and in hollows
under the rocks. The valley continues to the north and south.
name: Valley between Boulder-strewn Hillsides

desc:
The land is flat and open here, a wide expanse of tall, leaf-adorned stalks and
stems of growing things. A few trees grow here and there, and an occasional
thicket sometimes interrupts the grassy meadow. This wild place is the perfect
habitat for animals, birds, and insects.
name: Wide-open Meadow

desc:
The remains of a stone building can be seen here where the narrow track bends
before entering some hills. Only a few walls left standing and whatever purpose
of the building that once had has now been forgotten. The surrounding lands are
covered in heath with the occasional island of bushes growing where they can.
name: Narrow Track beside a Ruin

desc:
Luscious, yellow flowers clamber up between circular leaves that float in this
black water. The lily pads are so abundant here, that the lands appears to be
one, great, flat field. The water comes up to mid-thigh on a man, and the land
that does rise up is so saturated as to provide sloppy footing. This footing
does seem to lead through the land, and if followed is found to cross from
southwest to northeast.
name: Edge of a Marsh

desc:
Silver-leaved hawthorn shrubs grow tall on this spongy ground, interspersed
under the wide canopies of crab apple trees. The ground is covered in mushy,
leaf litter, and is shot through thick with new shoots of ever-growing brush,
causing travel to be hindered and slow. Above, brown and white butterflies float
among the crab apple flowers, landing on the trees for a rest. Hidden in the
shade of the shrubs and trees is a meandering trail that runs from the southwest
to the northeast.
name: Tall Shrubs

desc:
Patches of purple heather linger on this high moorland, the hills large and
pitched. The rest of the ground is covered in a mat of starry flowers and black
berries that hide between needle-like leaves. The ground is wet and peaty,
springing back underfoot. Nary a sound is heard on these hills except for the
trickling of water.
name: Patchy Moorland

desc:
Bent, sparse, rock whitebeam trees cling to limestone overhangs on these hills.
The rocky land concedes patches of grasses in the flatter areas, but otherwise
is devoid of flora. Views from the hilltops allow a sight of the great marsh to
the southeast where these hills dramatically slide into a watery morass, and to
the north and west the hills drop off abruptly in staggering cliffs.
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
This narrow trail still sees occasional use as the surrounding vegetation has
not yet managed to cover it. To the east, a stand of willow trees rises out of
the plains and marks the western edge of the Midgewater. To the west a lush
grassland extends, providing a home to uncounted numbers of small animals. The
trail continues along the edge of the marsh to both the north and south.
name: Trail along the Marsh

desc:
This narrow trail bends sharply from south to east here, skirting a stand of
hardwood trees that has flourished against the Midgewater Marshes. To the west
and north extends a grass plain, seemingly teeming with small game.
name: Narrow Trail

desc:
The trails converge here into a crude intersection. To the west, the land rises
into some high moorlands, with a narrow, dusty trail heading in that direction.
A large, slightly wider trail bends south and east. This one is packed more
firm, evidence that it is well-travelled.
name: Bend in the Narrow Trail

desc:
Heaving green shrubs show their purple and yellow flowers on these high
moorlands. The land stretches in long, waving views in all directions,
eventually flattening to the east. To the south, the hills become sharp and
impassable, and to the west, drop off in ragged cliffs. From the north and
heading east is a lightly used dirt trail that is lost and found at many
junctures by all but the most wisened travellers.
name: High Moorlands

desc:
High moorlands stretch out in all directions, hilly and covered in heath. The
plants that grow here are hardy and thrive in the cold and wet environs. On
steep dropoffs, limestone is exposed and small, twisted trees grow, fighting for
life, able to escape the rampant shrubs. Running north and south through the
land is a lightly worn trail.
name: High Moorlands

desc:
Yellow and green gorse fight for life amid coarse brush in the hard packed dirt
on this open field. As far as the eye can see are plains, and only the hazy
outlines of hills are visible far to the east. The tips of a tall cliff can be
seen, rising above the plain very far to the south.
name: Gorse-strewn Field

desc:
Gorse, shrub, and buttercup scrounge for life on this wild plain. The ground is
hard and often bare, exuding the smell of soil. The plain extends in all
directions and only to the northeast is there any change of scenery: brown
foothills rising slowly to the east.
name: Wild Plain

desc:
Dense thorny cover spreads over this field. The plant is called gorse in the
common tongue, whereas rangers call it whin. Perpetually in bloom, its yellow
flowers cover the land in all directions, and move as an ocean when the northern
gales blow.
name: Gorse Field

desc:
This furrowed land gradually changes from a brush covered plain in the west to
golden hills, thus beginning what the elves call Emyn Sul. The beginnings of
these windswept hills are home to hardy herding animals and their predators. The
land is wild and becomes more so to the east where far off hills are seen.
name: Furrowed Land

desc:
These foothills furrow at abnormal angles, betraying some ancient disturbance,
perhaps a battle. Otherwise, the land generally rises to the east ever so
slightly, being covered in flora much undisturbed. The country is silent except
for the ruffling of animals and insects.
name: Sombre Foothills

desc:
This land slopes up from the west in an impressive vista, giving a grand view of
this high rolling plain. Hardy gorse grows in dense patches, filling in low
spots, while grasses cover the rest. Leeward of the gorse provides refuge for
insects and small animals.
name: High Rolling Plain

desc:
Yellow flowering gorse is prolific on this plain, growing wildly thick as it
approaches the cliffs south and east. Deer or elk can be heard bleating to their
young in the distance. North and west the plain continues unimpeded, the plant
life there being more meagre.
name: Wild Gorse Plain

desc:
Grasses cover these foothills in a great golden landscape. The grasses stretch
on and on, allowing an easterly view of hazy outlines of far off hills. To the
west, a steep dropping scarp would stop any beast or man from travelling there.
name: Golden Foothills

desc:
Large swaths of grasses are interrupted by an imposing brush-filled gully that
runs from the southwest. The gully tapers to a traversable ditch northeast over
this heath, splitting the land into two. An animal path is visible at the
shallow end of the ditch, and the sour smell of musk pervades the furrow. To the
east a formidable cliff seems untraversable.
name: Split Heath

desc:
The western and northern edges of this plain drop dramatically, forming a long
curving cliff. This is but the beginning of Emyn Sul, land of hardy shepherds
and wolves who face the harsh northern wind with determination. The plains begin
to roll, forming foothills that give a meagre life to grasses and shrubs.
name: Foothills

desc:
Two ancient pine trees bend like the earth's hands, blown over south by steady
northerlies. Their bases are provided some cover by a small exposed dirt and
rock cliff. The rest of this land is foothills low and sturdy covered in golden
grass. East of here a large cliff rises, which continues to form a high plain.
name: Two Bent Pines

desc:
Hardy herd animals forage on the thick grasses here. Juniper holds on stubbornly
in between rocky mounds that sit among the land. The land drops sharply far the
west and south, preventing travel there. The hills undulate to the east and
north.
name: Shallow Foothills

desc:
Stunted hills beget stunted trees and scraggly bushes. The land is sparse,
mostly grasses and hardy bush, but leeward of hills, bent wych elm and black
poplar huddle under cedar overhangs. A steep, rock-strewn scarp lies north. To
the south the lands gives way to a steep cliff.
name: Stunted Hills

desc:
This highland furrows in craggy knobs making travel arduous. Bent pine and
juniper fight off thick brush in rough knots. The land descends sharply in all
directions but north. A tall pine tree grows near the peak of the cliff,
providing a home for birds and insects.
name: Highland

desc:
Irregular sod gives way to packed earth, olive-coloured brush and spindly gorse.
To the west and north, the land is much the same. To the east, a brush ridden
cliff impedes travel. The land to the south rises sharply into a tall, rocky
peak.
name: Flat Plain

desc:
This trail, running from the Chetwood north alongside the western edge of the
Midgewater, here passes through a low area and consequently becomes very muddy.
Several shallow ditches enter the marshes to the east, and there are no bridges
along the trail. To the west of the trail, the land is much dryer and covered
with thick grasses.
name: Wet Trail

desc:
This trail is pressed flush against the western border of the Midgewater
Marshes. In many places, the reeds and sedges of the marsh actually intrude upon
the trail. The ground is extremely muddy, and trail is often cut by stretches of
shallow water (not suitable for drinking) over which there are no bridges.
name: Wet Trail

desc:
Rocky hills in the south transition to heath, relenting to hardy, purple and
yellow flowered brush. The rocky ground is hard, grey limestone, whereas the
heath covering is spongy, bouncing back any step. Winds blow cold and wet on the
highlands, and except for the wind, all else is quiet. North, east and south,
the hills drop off in formidable cliffs, allowing the only exit west.
name: Rocky and Heath-covered Hills

desc:
A narrow trail runs east and west, with a small scarp bordering it to the north.
The trail runs along the base of the scarp. To the south lies an area of damp
grassland, and beyond that the Midgewater Marshes. Sometimes the trail is damp
and muddy when it passes through a low area, but mostly it is dry and made of
packed earth.
name: Narrow Trail

desc:
This trail of packed earth runs eastward along the base of the scarp marking the
northern boundary of the Midgewater. To the south, it skirts the western edge of
the marsh and eventually enters the Chetwood.
name: Narrow Trail

desc:
Dense scrubs eke out a meagre existence on the hard earth. Brisk winds
frequently sweep across the plains, often bringing a chill from the north. There
is a small scarp immediately to the south, but travel in that direction is no
real challenge.
name: Windswept Plains

desc:
The land here is relatively flat, save for a slight elevation to the north that
gradually ascends into some rolling foothills. To the east, the land is flat and
covered in a thin, grassy carpet. There are few trees about, but the plains are
dominated by scraggly brush and wiry thorn bushes.
name: Windswept Plains

desc:
A gentle slope up from the southwest leads to the edge of this low bluff that
overlooks the tangled vegetation of the Midgewater Marshes. Directly below,
after a drop of about ten feet, the muddy pools and wet vegetation of the marsh
hold sway, but this hill provides a dry respite for travellers.
name: Low Bluff

desc:
Clouds of hungry insects are raised by each footstep, each seeking warm blood
with a seemingly malevolent intelligence. The air is filled with the buzzing and
chirping of insects, punctuated by the loud calls of frogs. The water is shallow
for the most part, but there are occasional pockets of black mud that seem to
have no bottom.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
Marsh grasses and alders dot the landscape as the swampy soil of the northern
marsh becomes firmer ground here at its southern edge. To the east and south,
low hillocks rise out of the marshes, but a steep escarpment hinders southern
travel. A light forest of oak and ash grows in the western distance.
name: Thinning Marsh

desc:
The low hills rise toward a small clearing surrounded by thick bushes and
willows of many species. The firm, grass-covered ground gives way to soft
patches of mud in certain places. To the north, a steep escarpment plunges
toward a marsh, while the eastern and southern directions are blocked by
impenetrable bushes and willow trees.
name: Small Glade near Some Marshes

desc:
A damp, decaying stench fills the air, accompanied by a cacophony of croaking
from unseen marsh frogs. The ground underfoot is soft, waterlogged, and none too
easy to wade through. In some areas, shallow brown puddles appear filled with
low bur-reed and tall bulrush.
name: Edge of the Midgewater Marshes

desc:
The ground is very wet here, almost swampy. Dense, small willows rise above the
marshes to the north, east, and south, and there is an especially thick stand of
reeds farther east. To the west, an open meadow stretches north and south and a
dense tree line marks the western horizon.
name: Willowy Marsh

desc:
The frequent stretches of deep mud and black ponds make it impossible to travel
in a given direction with any certainty. On all sides the marshes reign
unchallenged, providing a home to ravenous hordes of biting and stinging
insects, who in turn sustain a huge population of frogs and other less familiar
predators.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
The solid ground of the western field has become squidgy underfoot, and every
lifted footstep produces a soft squelching sound. Directly to the east lies a
thick and impenetrable mound of brushwood. The marsh is totally overgrown here.
The tendrils and thorns of the brushwood make any movement east inconceivable.
name: Soggy Edge of a Field

desc:
Sedges and bulrushes form walkable islands on the edge of a marsh. What ground
exists is very muddy and gives to any step. Flat green plants cover the water's
surface, while old stems stick through, dead and brown. To the west, where the
land rises, tall, green shrubs grow in a thick, entangling wall, stopping travel
in that direction.
name: Edge of a Marsh

desc:
Low rises form soggy islands that are overgrown with wide-leaved reedmace. The
surrounding shallow water is thick with wet, fuzzy hornwort that sloshes onto
any beast's foot that travels through. Sparingly, sickly, drowned pine trees
grow on the higher ground, their trunks black and their needles yellow and
drooping. The air is slightly sour and is abuzz with small, black bugs.
name: Edge of a Marsh

desc:
Insects create constant movement on the surface of the water that stagnates on
the edge of a marsh. Dark pools surround low rises of narrow-bladed bulrushes,
providing soggy footing for land bound travellers. In the water, choking plants
vie for sunlight as the rushes droop over. The land is loud with the chirping
sounds of frogs and crickets, and echoes sharply off the water.
name: Edge of a Marsh

desc:
The difficulties of navigating through this area are but a harbinger of the
troubles that await those who dare explore deeper into the marsh. Frequently,
treacherous pools of unknown depth are hidden beneath a cover of aquatic plants,
and what land there is is far from solid.
name: Edge of Marsh

desc:
Clouds of insects clog the air, making it impossible to breathe with comfort.
There is no way to travel in any one given direction with consistency, as the
pools of muddy water and treacherous mudbanks seem to shift and conspire to
prevent it.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
The marshes come to their end some distance to the west, as beyond the dense
vegetation the dark wall of the trees of the Chetwood can sometimes be made out.
Frogs and other small animals abound, flourishing on the veritable plague of
small insects that give these marshes their name.
name: Midgewater Marshes

desc:
An old trail leading westward into the Chetwood is clearly marked, but it is
soon lost in the eastward direction, swallowed by the marshes. The ground that
lies above the level of the many dark pools in this area is very damp - giving
slightly at each step.
name: End of Trail

desc:
A small raised ridge of earth along the border of the marsh provides a home for
a veritable tangle of young willow trees. To the south and east, the ground
drops and then levels off into the untracked fens of the Midgewater, while to
the north and west, a seldom-used trail runs.
name: Stand of Willows

desc:
Water six inches deep covers the black earth, providing sustenance to unnumbered
cattails. The ground beneath the water is a soupy organic sludge that sucks and
pulls with each step. All around are hordes of insects - most of whom seem to
bite or sing - that have taken advantage of the ideal environment provided by
the marsh.
name: Cattails

desc:
The relatively flat ground of this area supports a great quantity of high grass
that, judging from the spoor, in turn supports a large population of small
mammals. To the south and east lies the Midgewater Marshes. To the west, the
grass is dotted with small patches of hardwood.
name: Grassy Plain

desc:
The water table is just below the surface here, and footprints quickly fill with
water. Many varieties of sawgrass have flourished here on the northern edge of
the Midgewater Marshes, and care must be taken to avoid being cut by their
serrated blades. Circling this area along the northern edge, a small trail leads
from east to west.
name: Wet Grasslands

desc:
Only twelve to fifteen feet wide, this small ledge forms a rocky path, yet seems
quite safe to walk on. It leads along a dense wall of rock. Tiny bushes and wild
grass grow out of cracks in the surface of the rock in the wall as well as on
the path itself. Near the brink, the hard stone begins to crumble and break a
sign of the passage of time and weather.
name: On a Small Ledge

desc:
The trail running through this field has seen more interesting days when it was
travelled often. The fields stretch out a few miles to the south, west, and
east, mostly inhabited by grasses and small bushes. North of here, a long hedge
disappears off into the west and east as it runs beside the trail.
name: Fields along the Old East Road

desc:
Directly to the north, an impenetrable hedge blocks all progress. On this side
of the hedge, a small trail winds its way among the low rolling hills, running
parallel to the line of bushes and presumably mirroring the Old East Road
beyond.
name: Trail along the Hedge

desc:
The current of this sluggish stream is barely discernible as it lazily drains
southwestward into the Midgewater Marshes. The banks on both sides are covered
by young willows and small, stunted alder trees. The soupy bottom of the stream
lies only a few feet down, but the mud below seems to have no bottom, forcing
those who would cross to take care.
name: Muddy Stream
